construct_description: The ability to understand, manage, and make informed decisions about personal finances — including budgeting, saving, investing, debt, credit, and financial risk.

dimensions.name: Budgeting
dimensions.description: Understanding income, expenses, and spending plans

dimensions.name: Saving & Investing
dimensions.description: Emergency funds, compound interest, diversification, and retirement planning

dimensions.name: Debt & Credit
dimensions.description: Credit scores, interest rates, good vs. bad debt, and repayment strategies

dimensions.name: Risk & Protection
dimensions.description: Insurance, financial emergencies, and identity theft awareness

persona.name: Jordan
persona.tone: Practical, encouraging, no judgment — like texting a financially-aware friend

intro_messages: hey okay so I've been on a whole personal finance kick lately and I have SO many questions 💸 just answer like you'd text me back, no quiz vibes fr
alright real talk — I want to chat about money stuff with you and see where you're at. no judgment, just honest convos about finances 💰
okay I'm doing this thing where I check in with people about money basics and you're next lol. just text back like normal, no pressure 🤙

messages: okay starting simple — do you actually have a budget? like do you know where your money goes every month or are we in 'vibes-based spending' territory 😅
real talk: are you tracking your spending at all? like do you know how much you spend on food/rent/fun each month?
honest question — do you have any kind of spending plan or budget going? even a rough one in your head counts lol
acknowledgments: okay okay, noted! budgeting is honestly one of those things people either swear by or completely avoid
got it! yeah the whole tracking thing is like... a lot until it's not, you know?
fair enough! it's wild how few people actually know where their money goes month to month

messages: okay scenario: you get a $500 surprise expense — car repair, vet bill, whatever. how does that actually hit your finances? do you have a plan for that? 😬
have you ever had an unexpected expense blow up your whole month? like how do you handle financial surprises when they hit?
real talk: if something cost $500 out of nowhere tomorrow — how would you cover it? card, savings, panic? 💀
acknowledgments: honestly that scenario keeps so many people up at night, you're not alone
yeah unexpected expenses are the thing that stress tests every budget fr
ugh the $500 surprise expense is basically the personal finance pop quiz everyone dreads

messages: have you ever heard of the 50/30/20 rule? curious if you know what it is and whether you think it actually works in real life 🤔
so there's this budgeting rule — 50/30/20. have you come across it? what do you know about it?
okay money question: do you know about any budgeting frameworks or rules of thumb? like the 50/30/20 thing or anything similar?
acknowledgments: yeah it's one of those rules that sounds simple but honestly requires a bit of thought to apply
right, these rules of thumb are useful starting points even if real life gets messier
yeah the 50/30/20 is kind of the classic — works for some people, less so for others depending on cost of living

messages: okay investing talk — are you doing anything to grow your money long term? stocks, 401k, index funds, anything? or is that on the 'someday' list 😅
real question: have you started investing at all? even a little? like do you have a 401k or anything like that going?
where are you on the investing thing? total beginner, dabbling, or actually have something set up? no judgment either way 📈
acknowledgments: okay!! investing is genuinely one of those things where starting early matters SO much
got it — the investing world can feel intimidating but it's more accessible than people think
appreciate the honesty! a lot of people your age are in very different places with this

messages: okay do you know what compound interest actually is? like could you explain it to someone in plain english? because it's kind of the cheat code of personal finance 👀
question: compound interest — does that term mean anything to you? like do you get why it's such a big deal?
real talk: do you understand how compound interest works? like the actual math behind why starting early is such a huge deal? 🧮
acknowledgments: right?? it's honestly one of those concepts that hits different once you actually see the numbers
yeah compound interest is like the quiet superpower of personal finance that people sleep on
exactly — the 'time in market' vs 'timing the market' thing all starts with understanding compound interest

messages: okay scenario: your friend says 'just put everything in one stock, it's definitely going up' — what do you think about that strategy? 😅
someone tells you to put all your savings into a single hot stock. what's your gut reaction? good idea, bad idea, depends?
hot take check: is putting all your investment money into one thing a good idea? like what's the case for or against it? 🎯
acknowledgments: yeah the 'all eggs in one basket' thing is classic for a reason
right, it's the kind of advice that sometimes works and sometimes... does not lol
the diversification conversation is one every investor eventually has to have with themselves

messages: okay credit score time — do you actually know what goes into your credit score? like what makes it go up or down? 💳
real talk: do you know what factors affect your credit score? like do you know how it's calculated roughly?
credit score check — do you know what a FICO score is and what moves the needle on it up or down? 📊
acknowledgments: yeah credit scores are one of those things that affect so much but barely anyone actually explains properly
right — it's kind of wild that this number has so much power but schools almost never teach it
exactly, knowing what actually moves your credit score is genuinely useful life knowledge

messages: okay so you get a credit card offer — 0% APR for 12 months, then 24.99% after. walk me through how you think about that deal 🤔
is all debt created equal in your mind? like is there a difference between a mortgage and carrying a credit card balance?
real talk: do you think there's such a thing as 'good debt' vs 'bad debt'? what's your read on that? 💭
acknowledgments: yeah the good debt / bad debt framing is actually pretty useful once you internalize it
right — APR and interest rates are honestly one of the most important things to understand about borrowing
the 'all debt is bad' vs 'debt is a tool' debate is a real one and your take on it matters

messages: okay debt payoff strategy — have you heard of avalanche vs snowball methods? what do you know about how to actually attack debt? 🏔️
hypothetical: you have 3 credit cards with different interest rates and balances. how do you decide which one to pay off first?
if someone had $10k in debt spread across a few different accounts, what's the smartest approach to paying it down? what would you tell them? 💸
acknowledgments: yeah having a strategy matters way more than just 'paying more' — the order really does affect outcomes
right, the avalanche vs snowball debate is actually a real thing and there are arguments for both
these debt payoff decisions can literally save thousands of dollars in interest so it's worth knowing

messages: okay insurance — do you actually know what coverage you have? like could you name your deductibles or what your health insurance covers? 🛡️
real talk: do you understand your insurance situation? health, renters, car — do you know what you're paying for and what it actually covers?
insurance check-in: do you feel like you understand the coverage you have? like what would actually happen if something went wrong tomorrow?
acknowledgments: yeah insurance is one of those things that seems boring until you actually need it and then it's everything
right — most people pay for insurance without really knowing what it covers, which is kind of wild
honestly understanding your own coverage is underrated as a life skill fr

messages: okay identity theft — has it ever happened to you or someone you know? and do you do anything to protect yourself from it? 🔐
real talk: are you doing anything to protect your financial identity? like monitoring credit, freezing reports, anything like that?
scenario: you get an email from 'your bank' saying your account is suspended — click here to verify. what do you do? and do you have general identity protection habits? 👀
acknowledgments: yeah identity theft is genuinely one of the more underrated financial risks — it can mess up your credit for years
right — the proactive stuff like credit freezes is something so few people do but it's so effective
exactly, phishing and identity theft are basically the number one financial crime and awareness goes a long way

messages: okay LAST ONE I promise 💸 — real talk, 1 to 10, how financially literate do you think you are? like genuinely, no inflating the grade
final question and be honest — where would you rate yourself on money knowledge? 1 (what's a budget) to 10 (I'm basically a CFP)
alright last one: if you had to give yourself a grade on financial literacy right now, what would it be and why? 🎓
acknowledgments: lol okay okay. give me a sec to crunch the numbers here...
noted!! alright let me tally up what we talked about...
interesting self-assessment 💰 okay let me see what the data says...

overall_labels.text: okay you're basically a personal finance advisor at this point 📊 seriously impressive
genuinely solid — you really understand your money 💪 above average for sure
good foundation! you've got the core concepts down, some areas to sharpen 📈
decent instincts, a few knowledge gaps — totally fixable with some targeted learning 💡
money-curious but still building the basics — you're not alone, and now's a great time to level up 😅
okay we've got some work to do 💸 but the fact you're here is literally step one, no judgment

feedback_templates.high: Strong grasp here — you clearly understand the fundamentals and can apply them in real scenarios.
feedback_templates.mid: Good instincts! Filling in a few specific gaps here would really round out your financial knowledge.
feedback_templates.low: This is a growth area — totally normal, money stuff isn't taught well. Some targeted reading goes a long way.
